examples.explanation: Array: [2,3,4,5,6], n=5. Initialize count=0. Pair (0,1): gcd(2,3)=1 → count=1. Pair (0,2): gcd(2,4)=2 → no. Pair (0,3): gcd(2,5)=1 → count=2. Pair (0,4): gcd(2,6)=2 → no. Pair (1,2): gcd(3,4)=1 → count=3. Pair (1,3): gcd(3,5)=1 → count=4. Pair (1,4): gcd(3,6)=3 → no. Pair (2,3): gcd(4,5)=1 → count=5. Pair (2,4): gcd(4,6)=2 → no. Pair (3,4): gcd(5,6)=1 → count=6. Total pairs checked: 10, coprime pairs: 6.

Array: [1,2,3,4], n=4. Initialize count=0. Pair (0,1): gcd(1,2)=1 → count=1. Pair (0,2): gcd(1,3)=1 → count=2. Pair (0,3): gcd(1,4)=1 → count=3. Pair (1,2): gcd(2,3)=1 → count=4. Pair (1,3): gcd(2,4)=2 → no. Pair (2,3): gcd(3,4)=1 → count=5. Total pairs checked: 6, coprime pairs: 5.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n² log M)
approaches.summary: Iterate over all pairs in the array and compute their GCD using Euclid's algorithm, counting those with GCD = 1. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Coprime Pairs with Brute Force
ladders.desc: Count all pairs (i,j) where i<j and gcd(arr[i], arr[j]) = 1 using O(n² log M) brute force approach.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to the problem using the brute force approach. The intuition is straightforward: we need to examine every possible pair in the array and check if they are coprime (gcd = 1). Since we're counting pairs where i<j, we avoid counting duplicates. The algorithm uses nested loops: the outer loop iterates through each element as the first element of a pair, and the inner loop iterates through subsequent elements to form pairs. For each pair, we compute their GCD using Euclid's algorithm. If the GCD equals 1, we increment our count. The time complexity is O(n² log M) where n is array length and M is the maximum value in the array (since Euclid's algorithm takes O(log M) time). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a counter variable. This approach is conceptually simple but becomes inefficient for large n due to the quadratic time complexity. Common mistakes include: counting pairs where i≥j (leading to duplicates), forgetting to handle edge cases like empty arrays or single-element arrays, and incorrect GCD implementation. The connection to L1 is that this level uses the GCD computation as a key subroutine. Real code pattern: initialize count = 0; for i from 0 to n-2: for j from i+1 to n-1: if gcd(arr[i], arr[j]) == 1: count += 1; return count.

examples.trace: Array: [2,3,4,5,6], n=5. Initialize count=0. Pair (0,1): gcd(2,3)=1 → count=1. Pair (0,2): gcd(2,4)=2 → no. Pair (0,3): gcd(2,5)=1 → count=2. Pair (0,4): gcd(2,6)=2 → no. Pair (1,2): gcd(3,4)=1 → count=3. Pair (1,3): gcd(3,5)=1 → count=4. Pair (1,4): gcd(3,6)=3 → no. Pair (2,3): gcd(4,5)=1 → count=5. Pair (2,4): gcd(4,6)=2 → no. Pair (3,4): gcd(5,6)=1 → count=6. Total pairs checked: 10, coprime pairs: 6.

Array: [1,2,3,4], n=4. Initialize count=0. Pair (0,1): gcd(1,2)=1 → count=1. Pair (0,2): gcd(1,3)=1 → count=2. Pair (0,3): gcd(1,4)=1 → count=3. Pair (1,2): gcd(2,3)=1 → count=4. Pair (1,3): gcd(2,4)=2 → no. Pair (2,3): gcd(3,4)=1 → count=5. Total pairs checked: 6, coprime pairs: 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute GCD using Euclid's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement Euclid's algorithm to compute the greatest common divisor of two integers.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function for the brute force approach. The intuition behind Euclid's algorithm is based on the mathematical property that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). This recursive relationship allows us to reduce the problem size with each step until we reach the base case where b=0, at which point gcd(a,0)=a. The algorithm works by repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder of dividing the larger by the smaller. This is efficient because the numbers decrease quickly. The time complexity is O(log min(a,b)) since the numbers reduce exponentially. Space complexity is O(log min(a,b)) for the recursive version due to call stack, or O(1) for iterative implementation. Common mistakes include: not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite recursion if not checking b=0, and integer overflow in languages with fixed-size integers. The connection to L0 is that this GCD computation is called for every pair in the brute force solution. Real code pattern (iterative): while b != 0: a, b = b, a % b; return abs(a).

examples.trace: Step 1: a=48, b=18. Since b≠0: compute 48 % 18 = 12. Update a=18, b=12. Step 2: b≠0: 18 % 12 = 6. Update a=12, b=6. Step 3: b≠0: 12 % 6 = 0. Update a=6, b=0. Step 4: b=0, return a=6.
Step 1: a=17, b=13. b≠0: 17 % 13 = 4. Update a=13, b=4. Step 2: b≠0: 13 % 4 = 1. Update a=4, b=1. Step 3: b≠0: 4 % 1 = 0. Update a=1, b=0. Step 4: b=0, return a=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Over All Unique Pairs in Array
ladders.desc: Generate all unique index pairs (i,j) where i<j from an array.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique for the brute force approach: systematically generating all possible pairs without duplication. The intuition is that for an array of n elements, there are n*(n-1)/2 unique pairs where the first index is less than the second. We use nested loops where the outer loop variable i goes from 0 to n-2, and the inner loop variable j goes from i+1 to n-1. This ensures that for each pair (i,j), we have i<j, and we never repeat pairs in reverse order. The time complexity is O(n²) for the pair generation itself. Space complexity is O(1) if we just process pairs without storing them. Common mistakes include: using j starting from 0 (which counts pairs twice), using j ≤ i (which includes pairs where i=j), and off-by-one errors in loop bounds. The connection to L0 is that this pair generation forms the main structure of the brute force algorithm, with GCD computation applied to each pair. Real code pattern: for i in range(0, n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): process pair (arr[i], arr[j]).

examples.trace: Array indices: 0,1,2. n=3. Outer loop i=0: inner loop j from 1 to 2: j=1 → pair (A,B); j=2 → pair (A,C). Outer loop i=1: inner loop j from 2 to 2: j=2 → pair (B,C). Outer loop i=2: not executed since i goes to n-2=1. Total pairs: 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Check If Two Numbers Are Coprime
ladders.desc: Determine if two integers are coprime (gcd = 1) using their GCD.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed to bridge GCD computation with pair counting. Two numbers are coprime (or relatively prime) if their greatest common divisor is 1. The intuition is simple: if the largest integer that divides both numbers is 1, then they share no common factors other than 1. After computing the GCD using Euclid's algorithm, we just check if the result equals 1. This is a boolean condition that can be used in conditional statements. The time complexity is dominated by the GCD computation: O(log min(a,b)). Space complexity is O(1) for the check itself. Common mistakes include: confusing coprime with prime numbers (coprime refers to pairs, not individual numbers), forgetting that 1 is coprime with every integer, and not handling edge cases like zero (gcd(0,x)=|x|, so 0 and x are coprime only if |x|=1). The connection to L1 is that this uses the GCD function as a building block. Real code pattern: return gcd(a, b) == 1.

examples.trace: Compute gcd(8,15). 15 % 8 = 7. 8 % 7 = 1. 7 % 1 = 0. gcd=1. Since 1 == 1, return True. Numbers are coprime.
Compute gcd(12,18). 18 % 12 = 6. 12 % 6 = 0. gcd=6. Since 6 != 1, return False. Numbers are not coprime.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Remainder (Modulo Operation)
ladders.desc: Calculate a mod b: the remainder when a is divided by b.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used repeatedly in Euclid's algorithm. The modulo operation, written as a % b, returns the remainder when a is divided by b. For example, 7 % 3 = 1 because 7 divided by 3 gives quotient 2 and remainder 1. The intuition is that it tells us how much is left over after dividing a by b as many times as possible. In Euclid's algorithm, we use a % b to reduce the problem size: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a % b). Key properties: 0 ≤ (a % b) < b when b > 0; a % 0 is undefined; and for negative numbers, definitions vary by language (typically follow dividend sign). Time complexity is O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include: assuming a % b is the same as a - b (only true when a < b), not handling b=0 cases, and confusion with integer division. The connection to L1 is that this operation is the core step in Euclid's algorithm. Real code pattern: result = a % b.

examples.trace: 17 divided by 5: 5*3=15, remainder = 17-15=2. So 17 % 5 = 2.
10 divided by 3: 3*3=9, remainder = 10-9=1. So 10 % 3 = 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Definition
ladders.desc: Understand what GCD means: the largest integer that divides two numbers without remainder.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for the entire problem. The greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both a and b without leaving a remainder. For example, gcd(12,18)=6 because 6 divides both 12 and 18, and no larger number does. The intuition is about finding common factors: list all divisors of each number, find the intersection, and take the maximum. Key properties: gcd(a,0)=|a|; gcd(a,b)=gcd(b,a); if gcd(a,b)=1, the numbers are coprime. This concept is foundational because the problem asks to count pairs with gcd=1. Understanding GCD helps in grasping why Euclid's algorithm works and what 'coprime' means. Common mistakes include: confusing GCD with LCM (least common multiple), thinking gcd(a,b) is always smaller than a and b (not true if a divides b), and not considering negative numbers (gcd is defined for negatives as gcd(|a|,|b|)). The connection to L3 is that knowing GCD definition allows checking if numbers are coprime. Real code pattern: conceptually, but for implementation we use Euclid's algorithm.

examples.trace: Divisors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12. Divisors of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18. Common divisors: 1,2,3,6. Largest common divisor: 6.
Divisors of 7: 1,7. Divisors of 5: 1,5. Common divisors: 1. Largest common divisor: 1.

approaches.name: Inclusion-Exclusion with Möbius Function O(n + M log M)
approaches.summary: Use the Möbius function to apply inclusion-exclusion on divisors, counting pairs where both numbers are divisible by a common divisor and subtracting overcounts. This leverages number theory to avoid pairwise checks.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count coprime pairs using Möbius function
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting pairs (i,j) with gcd(arr[i], arr[j]) = 1 using inclusion-exclusion with Möbius function
ladders.explanation: This solution efficiently counts coprime pairs without checking all O(n²) pairs directly. The core insight is that we can count pairs where both numbers share a common divisor d, then use the Möbius function μ(d) to apply inclusion-exclusion through the formula: count_coprime = Σ μ(d) * C(cnt[d], 2), where cnt[d] counts how many numbers in the array are divisible by d. The Möbius function μ(d) equals 0 if d has a squared prime factor, 1 if d has an even number of distinct prime factors, and -1 if d has an odd number of distinct prime factors. This automatically handles the inclusion-exclusion principle for all divisors. First, we find the maximum value M in the array. We compute cnt[d] for all d from 1 to M by iterating through the array and incrementing cnt[x] for each number x, then for each divisor d of x (d > 1), we also increment cnt[d]. This can be done efficiently by iterating d from 1 to M and for multiples m = d, 2d, 3d, ... ≤ M, adding cnt[m] to cnt[d]. Next, we precompute the Möbius function μ(d) for all d up to M using a modified sieve. Initialize μ[1] = 1. For each prime p, μ[p] = -1. For composite numbers, μ[d] = 0 if d is divisible by p², otherwise μ[d] = -μ[d/p]. Finally, we sum μ(d) * C(cnt[d], 2) for all d from 1 to M. C(cnt[d], 2) = cnt[d] * (cnt[d] - 1) / 2 counts pairs where both numbers are divisible by d. The sum gives the total coprime pairs because μ(d) ensures proper inclusion-exclusion: divisors with μ(d) = 1 add pairs, those with μ(d) = -1 subtract overcounts, and μ(d) = 0 skip numbers with squared prime factors. Time complexity is O(n + M log M) for computing cnt[d] and the sieve. Space is O(M). The connection to L1 is that this uses the Möbius function computation as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle large numbers in combinatorial calculations, incorrect Möbius computation, or inefficient divisor counting. Use integer arithmetic for combinations to avoid floating-point errors.

examples.trace: Step 1: M = max(arr) = 6. Initialize cnt[1..6] = 0. Process array: cnt[2]++, cnt[3]++, cnt[4]++, cnt[5]++, cnt[6]++. Step 2: Compute cnt[d] for divisors: For d=1, multiples 1,2,3,4,5,6: cnt[1] = cnt[1]+cnt[2]+cnt[3]+cnt[4]+cnt[5]+cnt[6] = 0+1+1+1+1+1 = 5. For d=2, multiples 2,4,6: cnt[2] = 1+1+1 = 3. For d=3, multiples 3,6: cnt[3] = 1+1 = 2. For d=4, multiple 4: cnt[4] = 1. For d=5, multiple 5: cnt[5] = 1. For d=6, multiple 6: cnt[6] = 1. Step 3: Compute Möbius μ[1..6]: μ[1]=1. For primes: μ[2]=-1, μ[3]=-1, μ[5]=-1. For composites: 4=2², so μ[4]=0. 6=2*3, distinct primes count=2 (even), so μ[6]=1. Step 4: Sum μ(d)*C(cnt[d],2): For d=1: μ=1, cnt=5, C(5,2)=10, contribution=10. d=2: μ=-1, cnt=3, C(3,2)=3, contribution=-3. d=3: μ=-1, cnt=2, C(2,2)=1, contribution=-1. d=4: μ=0, cnt=1, C(1,2)=0, contribution=0. d=5: μ=-1, cnt=1, C(1,2)=0, contribution=0. d=6: μ=1, cnt=1, C(1,2)=0, contribution=0. Total = 10 - 3 - 1 = 6. Verify: Pairs with gcd=1: (2,3), (2,5), (3,4), (3,5), (4,5), (5,6) = 6 pairs.

Step 1: M = 4. cnt[1]=1, cnt[2]=1, cnt[3]=1, cnt[4]=1. Step 2: Compute cnt[d]: d=1: multiples 1,2,3,4: cnt[1]=4. d=2: multiples 2,4: cnt[2]=2. d=3: multiple 3: cnt[3]=1. d=4: multiple 4: cnt[4]=1. Step 3: Möbius: μ[1]=1, μ[2]=-1, μ[3]=-1, μ[4]=0 (since 4=2²). Step 4: Sum: d=1: μ=1, cnt=4, C(4,2)=6, contribution=6. d=2: μ=-1, cnt=2, C(2,2)=1, contribution=-1. d=3: μ=-1, cnt=1, C(1,2)=0, contribution=0. d=4: μ=0, cnt=1, C(1,2)=0, contribution=0. Total=5. Pairs: (1,2), (1,3), (1,4), (2,3), (3,4) = 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Möbius function using sieve
ladders.desc: Precompute the Möbius function μ(d) for all d up to a maximum M using a modified sieve of Eratosthenes
ladders.explanation: The Möbius function μ(d) is crucial for inclusion-exclusion in number theory. For a positive integer d, μ(d) is defined as: 0 if d has a squared prime factor (i.e., is divisible by p² for some prime p), 1 if d is square-free with an even number of distinct prime factors, and -1 if d is square-free with an odd number of distinct prime factors. This function helps invert divisor sums via the Möbius inversion formula. To compute μ(d) for all d from 1 to M efficiently, we use a linear sieve similar to prime sieving. Initialize an array mu of size M+1 with mu[1] = 1. Maintain a list of primes and an array spf (smallest prime factor) for each number. Iterate i from 2 to M. If i is prime (spf[i] == 0), set mu[i] = -1 (since i has one distinct prime factor, odd). For each prime p in primes list such that p * i ≤ M: If p divides i, then p² divides p*i, so mu[p*i] = 0. Otherwise, mu[p*i] = -mu[i] (since adding a new prime factor flips the sign). This runs in O(M) time and O(M) space. The intuition is that we build mu dynamically using the multiplicative property: μ(ab) = μ(a)μ(b) if gcd(a,b)=1, and μ(p²k)=0. The sieve ensures each number is processed once. Connection to L0: This subroutine provides the μ(d) values needed in the main sum. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization, not handling mu[1]=1, or off-by-one errors in array bounds. Use integer arrays and careful indexing.

examples.trace: Initialize mu[1]=1, primes=[], spf all 0. i=2: prime, mu[2]=-1, primes=[2]. For p=2: p*i=4 ≤10, since p divides i? 2 divides 2, yes, so mu[4]=0. i=3: prime, mu[3]=-1, primes=[2,3]. For p=2: 6≤10, p=2 does not divide 3, so mu[6]=-mu[3]=1. For p=3: 9≤10, p divides i? 3 divides 3, yes, mu[9]=0. i=4: not prime (spf[4]=2), skip prime check. For p=2: 8≤10, p divides i? 2 divides 4, yes, mu[8]=0. i=5: prime, mu[5]=-1, primes=[2,3,5]. For p=2: 10≤10, p=2 does not divide 5, mu[10]=-mu[5]=1. For p=3: 15>10 stop. i=6: not prime. For p=2: 12>10 stop. i=7: prime, mu[7]=-1. i=8: not prime. i=9: not prime. i=10: not prime. Result: mu[1]=1, mu[2]=-1, mu[3]=-1, mu[4]=0, mu[5]=-1, mu[6]=1, mu[7]=-1, mu[8]=0, mu[9]=0, mu[10]=1.

Initialize mu[1]=1. i=2: prime, mu[2]=-1, primes=[2]. p=2: 4≤12, p divides i, mu[4]=0. i=3: prime, mu[3]=-1, primes=[2,3]. p=2: 6≤12, p not divide 3, mu[6]=-mu[3]=1. p=3: 9≤12, p divides i, mu[9]=0. i=4: not prime. p=2: 8≤12, p divides i, mu[8]=0. i=5: prime, mu[5]=-1, primes=[2,3,5]. p=2: 10≤12, p not divide 5, mu[10]=-mu[5]=1. p=3: 15>12 stop. i=6: not prime. p=2: 12≤12, p divides i? 2 divides 6, yes, mu[12]=0. i=7: prime, mu[7]=-1. i=8-12: continue similarly. Final mu as above.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Inclusion-exclusion using divisor counts
ladders.desc: Count pairs with gcd=1 by summing μ(d) * C(cnt[d], 2) over divisors d, where cnt[d] counts numbers divisible by d
ladders.explanation: The central technique avoids checking all pairs by using number theory. For any pair (a,b), gcd(a,b)=1 if and only if they share no common prime factors. Instead of testing pairs directly, we count pairs that are divisible by each divisor d, then use the Möbius function to apply inclusion-exclusion. Let cnt[d] be the number of elements in the array divisible by d. Then C(cnt[d], 2) = cnt[d]*(cnt[d]-1)/2 counts pairs where both numbers are multiples of d. The sum S = Σ μ(d) * C(cnt[d], 2) over d from 1 to M gives the count of coprime pairs. Why? Consider a pair (a,b) with gcd g. This pair is counted in C(cnt[d], 2) for every divisor d of g. The Möbius inversion ensures that the contribution of this pair to S is 1 if g=1 and 0 otherwise. Specifically, Σ_{d|g} μ(d) = 1 if g=1, else 0. Thus, summing over all pairs, each pair with gcd=1 contributes 1, others contribute 0. The algorithm: compute cnt[d] efficiently by first counting frequency of each number, then for each d, sum cnt[m] for multiples m of d. Then compute μ(d) via sieve. Finally, compute S. Time O(M log M) for the divisor sum step, space O(M). Connection to L1: This uses μ(d) from the Möbius computation. Common mistakes include incorrect combinatorial calculation (use integer division), missing d=1, or not understanding the inversion formula. Ensure cnt[d] is computed correctly for all d.

ladders.title: Building Block — Count numbers divisible by each divisor
ladders.desc: Given an array, compute cnt[d] = count of numbers divisible by d for all d up to maximum M
ladders.explanation: This intermediate step is essential for the inclusion-exclusion. We need to know, for each divisor d, how many numbers in the array are multiples of d. A naive approach would check each number against each d, taking O(n*M). Instead, we use a frequency-based method. Let freq[x] be the count of times number x appears in the array, for x from 1 to M. Then cnt[d] = Σ_{k=1}^{⌊M/d⌋} freq[k*d]. This sums frequencies of all multiples of d. Algorithm: First, find M = max(arr). Initialize freq array of size M+1 with zeros. Iterate through arr, increment freq[arr[i]]. Then, initialize cnt array of size M+1 with zeros. For d from 1 to M, iterate multiples m = d, 2d, 3d, ... ≤ M, and add freq[m] to cnt[d]. This runs in O(M log M) time because the total iterations are M*(1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + ...) ≈ M log M. Space is O(M). The intuition is that we leverage the harmonic series to efficiently aggregate counts. Connection to L2: This provides cnt[d] used in the core sum. Common mistakes include not initializing arrays properly, off-by-one errors, or inefficient double loops. Use nested loops with step d. Example: if freq[2]=1, freq[4]=1, freq[6]=1, then for d=2, cnt[2] = freq[2]+freq[4]+freq[6]=3.

examples.trace: Step 1: freq[1]=0, freq[2]=1, freq[3]=1, freq[4]=1, freq[5]=1, freq[6]=1. Step 2: Compute cnt[d]. d=1: multiples 1,2,3,4,5,6: cnt[1]=0+1+1+1+1+1=5. d=2: multiples 2,4,6: cnt[2]=1+1+1=3. d=3: multiples 3,6: cnt[3]=1+1=2. d=4: multiple 4: cnt[4]=1. d=5: multiple 5: cnt[5]=1. d=6: multiple 6: cnt[6]=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute frequency of array elements
ladders.desc: Count how many times each integer appears in an array up to a maximum value M
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves creating a frequency array freq where freq[x] is the number of occurrences of x in the input array. This is a basic histogram computation. Given an array arr of n integers and a maximum value M (which can be found by scanning the array), we initialize an array freq of size M+1 with all zeros. Then, iterate through each element val in arr: if val is within 1..M (assuming positive integers as in the problem), increment freq[val]. This runs in O(n) time and O(M) space. The intuition is simple: we're counting occurrences directly using array indexing for constant-time access. This operation is foundational for many algorithms that rely on value distributions. Connection to L3: The frequency array is used to compute cnt[d] by summing over multiples. Common mistakes include not allocating enough space (size M+1 to include index M), assuming values start at 0 instead of 1, or not handling large M efficiently. Always validate that values are within bounds. Example: for arr=[2,3,2,4], M=4, freq[2]=2, freq[3]=1, freq[4]=1, others 0.

examples.trace: Initialize freq[0..4]=0. Process arr: val=1: freq[1]++ → freq[1]=1. val=2: freq[2]++ → freq[2]=1. val=2: freq[2]++ → freq[2]=2. val=3: freq[3]++ → freq[3]=1. val=4: freq[4]++ → freq[4]=1. Result: freq[0]=0, freq[1]=1, freq[2]=2, freq[3]=1, freq[4]=1.
freq size 11. val=5: freq[5]=1. val=5: freq[5]=2. val=10: freq[10]=1. val=5: freq[5]=3. All others 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Greatest common divisor (GCD) and coprime numbers
ladders.desc: Understand gcd and when two numbers are coprime (gcd=1)
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept. The greatest common divisor (gcd) of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both a and b. For example, gcd(12,18)=6. Two numbers are called coprime (or relatively prime) if their gcd is 1, meaning they share no common prime factors. For instance, 8 and 15 are coprime because gcd(8,15)=1, even though neither is prime. This concept is fundamental in number theory and many algorithms. To compute gcd, you can use the Euclidean algorithm: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b), with base case gcd(a,0)=a. For example, gcd(48,18): gcd(48,18)=gcd(18,48 mod 18=12)=gcd(12,18 mod 12=6)=gcd(6,12 mod 6=0)=6. For coprime check, simply compute gcd; if result is 1, they are coprime. Intuition: Numbers are coprime if their prime factorizations have no primes in common. Connection to L4: In the main problem, we need to count pairs with gcd=1. Common mistakes include confusing coprime with prime numbers, or not handling zero correctly (gcd(0,a)=a, but in this problem numbers are positive). Always use positive integers. Example: 14 and 25 are coprime because gcd(14,25)=1.

examples.trace: Compute gcd(8,15): 15 mod 8 = 7. gcd(8,7): 8 mod 7 = 1. gcd(7,1): 7 mod 1 = 0. gcd(1,0)=1. So gcd=1, thus coprime.
gcd(12,18): 18 mod 12=6. gcd(12,6): 12 mod 6=0. gcd(6,0)=6. So gcd=6, not coprime.
